figures.caption: Figure 1: Architecture of WLNO. (a) Full pipeline: the input function f(x, t) is concatenated with spatial grid coordinates (ξ1, ξ2) and lifted to latent space v ∈ Rdz by P . The WLNO layer processes v through two parallel branches, PR2d Laplace and Haar DWT wavelet, whose outputs are fused via the learnable weight αwav and added to the local bypass Wloc. The result u is projected to the output field y(x, t) by Q. (b) WLNO layer detail: instancenormalized inputs enter the PR2d branch (computing transient exponential sums and steady-state IFFT responses) and the WaveletLayer2d branch (Haar DWT→ independent 1× 1 convolutions per subband→ IDWT) in parallel. Outputs are fused with learned weight αwav, normalized, and added to the 1× 1 Conv2d bypass.

Figure 2: Prediction comparison for one representative diffusion equation test sample, showing the input f(x, t), ground truth, LNO and WLNO predictions, and their corresponding absolute error maps. WLNO eliminates the systematic horizontal banding errors visible in the LNO error map.
Figure 3: Test error (mean ± std) over 130 diffusion equation test samples. WLNO reduces mean error by 32.3% and standard deviation by 8.4% relative to LNO.

Figure 4: Training and validation history for the Burgers equation over 1000 epochs. WLNO maintains lower loss values than LNO throughout all learning rate plateaus, with step-wise drops occurring at 100-epoch intervals due to the learning rate schedule.

Figure 5: Prediction comparison for two representative Burgers equation test samples, showing ground truth, LNO and WLNO predictions, and absolute error maps. WLNO produces sharper shock interfaces with significantly reduced errors.

Figure 6: Test error (mean ± std) over 100 Burgers equation test samples. WLNO reduces mean error by 22.8% and standard deviation by 39.3% relative to LNO, confirming that wavelet detail subbands are most effective when the solution contains sharp shock fronts.

Figure 7: Prediction comparison for one representative reaction-diffusion test sample, showing the ground truth, LNO and WLNO predictions, and their absolute error maps. Dominant errors in both models are concentrated near high-concentration regions generated by the ky2 reaction term.

Figure 8: Test error (mean ± std) over 130 reaction-diffusion test samples. WLNO reduces mean error by 14.1% and standard deviation by 11.4% relative to LNO, reflecting a more modest gain consistent with the temporally dominated nature of this PDE.

Figure 9: Prediction comparison for a representative Darcy flow test sample, showing the input permeability field a(x, y), ground truth pressure u(x, y), LNO and WLNO predictions, and their absolute error maps. WLNO reduces the spatially scattered residuals along the permeability discontinuity boundaries.
Figure 10: Test error (mean ± std) over the Darcy flow test samples. WLNO reduces mean error by 12.9% and standard deviation by 20.3% relative to LNO.

Figure 11: Prediction comparison for one representative Navier-Stokes test sample, showing the input forcing field f , vorticity at later time ω(T ), LNO and WLNO predictions, and their absolute error maps. WLNO produces lower and more spatially uniform residuals, particularly in regions of concentrated vorticity.

Figure 12: Test error (mean ± std) over the Navier-Stokes test samples. WLNO reduces mean error by 20.7% and standard deviation by 34.5% relative to LNO, demonstrating the benefit of wavelet spatial decomposition for turbulent flow operator learning.
